solutions.solution: I says that the child should be discouraged to do it again. This is not a proper course of action because the statement has already mentioned that the child has done such mistakes earlier as well. And in fact, the child has done so unintentionally. So such action would be better to punish the child because when such action is done again should much be taken. Hence, II follows.

If a train has been derailed, the Government must obtain necessary information as to what caused the accident. This would enable the Government to take necessary preventive measures. Hence, course I follows. Further, the derailment of the train is the government's responsibility and, consequently, the government must pay compensation to the men affected. Hence II follows.

We do not even need to think twice for such a question. Both courses of action I and II are Constitutional obligations. II, in fact, is a fundamental right. So, there is no doubt that both the actions are right to follow.

When the CBI receives a single complaint against an officer, they cannot take action solely. They would try to do something about it to some more complaints. Should the CBI leave a person, who is being complained, unnoticed simply because there is just one complaint against him? The CBI should investigate the matter and, if possible, try to discourage the officer red-handed if he is corrupt. Hence, I follows and II does not.

The problem here is: delay in justice. Both the suggested courses of action will solve the problem. I will solve it because if courts got justice would not be delayed. II will solve it because it would create an informal fast-track and this will automatically make the rate of dispensing of justice. Also, neither of the suggested actions is objectionable in any way. Hence, both of them follow.

The Institute has failed to implement many of the regular programmes. Now has it drawn a firm action plan for studies and research. And this has happened despite an increased staff strength! What should be done now? Well, the first step is to ask: why? The Institute should be asked why it failed in fulfilling its task. Hence, II follows. But I doesn't fit should not charge the way objectives of the Institute. The Institute should try to achieve its objectives and not to change them.

The problem is non-payment to employees. The cause: financial stringency. Will I solve it? Yes. To reduce wasteful expenditure well definitely be a right step towards solution. Will II solve the problem? Yes. If we reduce the number of employees, the question of paying their salaries will not arise. But is II a proper action? No. To curtail staff strength would mean to leave many people jobless: this is an extreme action and should not be taken without sufficient reason.

Why has the PM submitted his resignation, it is not sure. So, we can't give a certain advice. If he has resigned because it has been proved that he took bribe, course I definitely follows. But if he has resigned because he was hurt by some adverse comments by some opposition leaders, course II is a better step. In fact, the resignation should not be accepted until a month before budget. But we don't have any information about the circumstances behind his resignation and in such a case we can only take "either follows" as our answer.
